dateStatements.label: Translation
dateStatements.notes: Work-level estimate; the unique surviving manuscript was copied later.

entities.description: The anonymous Middle English verse translation of the Speculum humanae salvationis, probably made around 1400. It survives uniquely in Takamiya MS 97 and is distinct from the Latin source work, that manuscript's physical state, and Huth's reconstructed edition.
entities.label: The Mirour of Mans Saluacioun as an abstract Middle English work

rights.basis: The work was translated around 1400 and is attested in a circa-1450 manuscript, centuries before modern copyright. This assessment does not determine rights in Huth's edition typography, U-M markup, DIMEV data, Yale photography, or interfaces.
rights.component: Underlying medieval English work; modern editions, encodings, catalog data, and images excluded

dateStatements.label: Compilation
dateStatements.value: First quarter of the fourteenth century

entities.description: The anonymous Latin typological work, compiled in the first quarter of the fourteenth century, of which The Mirour of Mans Saluacioun is an English verse translation. This entity represents the work tradition, not an identified physical exemplar.
entities.label: Speculum humanae salvationis as an abstract Latin source work
notes: Huth compared the English text with a Zainer edition, Berjeau's block-book reproduction, and a circa-1370 Latin manuscript in his possession; he did not identify any as the exact English translation exemplar.

rights.basis: The anonymous Latin work was compiled in the first quarter of the fourteenth century. This historical text status does not assign rights to any modern scholarly edition or reproduction.
rights.component: Underlying fourteenth-century Latin work; modern editions, transcriptions, and images excluded

bibliographic.citation: Alfred Henry Huth, ed., The Miroure of Mans Saluacionne: A Fifteenth Century Translation into English of the Speculum humanae salvationis and now for the first time printed from a manuscript in the possession of Alfred Henry Huth, with preface and glossary (London: privately printed for the Roxburghe Club, 1888), xxix + 177 pp. + 1 plate.
bibliographic.publisher: Privately printed for the Roxburghe Club
bibliographic.title: The Miroure of Mans Saluacionne

entities.description: The privately printed Roxburghe Club edition containing a title page, dedication, preface, word list, errata, manuscript plate, Huth's edited text with glosses and supplies, manuscript index and damaged envoy, glossary, and endmatter. CME00118 selects only its title page and printed pp. 1-166.
entities.label: Alfred Henry Huth's 1888 The Miroure of Mans Saluacionne
notes: Only seventy-five copies were printed for the Roxburghe Club.

Huth transcribes the manuscript in principle but expands abbreviations in italics, applies stated i/j and nn/un decisions, restores obvious omissions in square brackets, adds glosses as footnotes, supplies eighty-four Middle English lines of his own, and prints one hundred Latin lines from the later supplement.
DIMEV v1.0's Huth1888 record names 'Henry Huth' as editor; the source volume identifies Alfred Henry Huth.

rights.basis: Huth's edition was first published in London in 1888; that publication year places the edition in the public domain in the United States under current term limits. Internet Archive also marks this exact item with the Public Domain Mark 1.0. This assessment does not imply that the edition was first published in the United States.
rights.component: 1888 printed edition, editorial matter, and typography as underlying source content; modern scan layer excluded

access.accessMethod: Register for Yale special-collections access, request Takamiya MS 97 through Quicksearch/Aeon at least two business days in advance, and consult it in the Beinecke reading room subject to current conditions
access.provider: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

access.accessMethod: Download existing files from Yale Digital Collections or submit an Aeon digitization request for preservation-quality files; all requests are reviewed for preservation and service limits
access.format: Existing TIFF, JPEG, or PDF downloads; requested TIFF or color PDF subject to current service terms
notes: The current reproduction page gives a 10-14 week turnaround and states that supplying a reproduction is not publication authorization. Exact cost, resolution, delivered completeness, and reuse conditions should be confirmed before ordering.
access.provider: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.notes: This dates the physical copy, not the English translation event.

entities.description: The unique surviving paper manuscript of the Middle English translation, copied in England around 1450 in one hand. The principal English work is at current ff. 1r-62v; an index occupies ff. 63r-64r, a damaged envoy f. 64v, and a later Latin supply f. 65r-v.

holding.notes: Purchased from Toshiyuki Takamiya on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund in 2017. The current manifest's Call Number field reads 'Takamiya MS 97 1', while its formal citation and scholarly records use Takamiya MS 97.

entities.label: Yale, Beinecke Library, Takamiya MS 97

physicalDescription.extent: 64 leaves; English work ff. 1r-62v; index ff. 63r-64r; damaged envoy f. 64v; Latin supplement f. 65r-v
physicalDescription.format: Bound manuscript, originally apparently four gatherings
notes: Christie's collation: 1^16, 2^15 (of 16, lacking ix), 3^16, 4^17 (of 20, final three probably blank), totaling 64 leaves.
Christie's records forty lines in cursive anglicana; Yale's migrated manifest says single columns of seventeen lines, an unresolved conflict with the visual object.
Medieval foliation runs 3-58 with duplicated 45 and verso 50 numbered 51; Christie's follows modern pencil pagination 1-130.
Red initials, line fillers, underlining, and other red marks structure the text; the binding is nineteenth-century full brown morocco gilt.
Provenance: Alfred Henry Huth; Sir Leicester Harmsworth; William and Christina Foyle; Toshiyuki Takamiya; Yale, 2017.

rights.basis: The manuscript was produced around 1450 and embodies medieval texts. Physical access, photography, supplied reproductions, catalog text, and Yale digital-image conditions are separate.
rights.component: Underlying medieval English and Latin texts embodied in the manuscript; physical ownership and digital images excluded

entities.description: A physical exemplar inferred from lacunae faithfully reflected by the Takamiya scribe. Huth and Christie's distinguish this damaged exemplar from leaves now missing or damaged in the extant manuscript; no shelfmark, date, location, or continued survival is known.
entities.label: Unidentified damaged exemplar copied by Takamiya MS 97
notes: The entity is intentionally unidentified. Its modeling prevents an exemplar lacuna from being mistaken for the separate physical leaf loss in the extant Takamiya manuscript.
At the lacuna represented on Huth pp. 154-157, the scribe left blank space because the exemplar lacked a leaf; a later owner copied a Latin version on a final leaf.
This inferred exemplar is not the abstract Latin source work and is not equated with any one Latin manuscript or print consulted by Huth.

next_steps: Consult Avril Henry's 1986 critical edition and later recension studies.
Collate the complete English text against the Latin witnesses and prints Huth names without converting any comparison source into a demonstrated exemplar.
Model the translation source, the English copy exemplar, and the extant Takamiya manuscript as separate layers unless passage-level evidence connects them.
openQuestions.question: Which Latin recension and physical manuscript exemplar underlay the Middle English translation, and how does that source differ from the damaged exemplar copied by Takamiya MS 97?

next_steps: Ask Beinecke cataloging staff to confirm whether the call-number suffix and seventeen-line statement are migration errors.
Map every canvas to physical leaves using the modern pagination and Christie's gathering collation.
Continue to describe the cache as complete relative to all 144 provider-published canvases rather than silently claiming a reconciled physical inventory.
openQuestions.question: How should Yale's 144 canvas labels, 'Takamiya MS 97 1' call-number string, and seventeen-line layout statement be reconciled with the 64-leaf collation, anomalous medieval foliation, modern pagination, and forty-line layout?

next_steps: Ask Kyvero Press to clarify the intended scope of the root Unlicense for source XML and derived data.
Add a path-level data/markup license or scope statement if the public-domain dedication is intended to cover those materials.
Until clarified, retain the exact license evidence without conflating it with U-M's No Copyright-United States statement or the medieval text's status.
openQuestions.question: Does the pinned CME repository's software-framed Unlicense declaration expressly cover corpus XML, encoded data, and markup such as CME00118.xml?

next_steps: Ask Beinecke Public Services for a shelfmark- and OID-specific image reuse statement for Takamiya MS 97.
Record any quoted contractual conditions separately from copyright status and physical ownership.
Do not infer a Public Domain Mark or Creative Commons license because the exact IIIF manifest supplies neither.
openQuestions.question: What item-specific reuse terms, if any, apply to the Yale IIIF image files beyond the manifest's general copyright/site-license caution and Beinecke's general policy?

relations.objectLabel: Alfred Henry Huth's 1888 The Miroure of Mans Saluacionne

scope.notes: The release's editor forename is erroneous; the cited bibliographic object is nevertheless the same 1888 edition.

reviewNotes: The primary subject is the repository artifact at CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00118.xml; the English work, Latin source tradition, Huth edition, U-M encoding, manuscript, inferred exemplar, and facsimiles are separate entities.

Takamiya MS 97 is the unique known witness of the Middle English version. Thomas Cowper and Thomas Wilkinson occur in contradictory late annotations; neither is promoted to author, translator, or scribe.

The XML represents Huth's title page and pp. 1-166 only. It excludes his dedication, preface, preliminary word list, errata, manuscript plate, the witness index printed pp. 167-170, damaged envoy p. 171, glossary pp. 173-177, and printer/endmatter.

The XML has 5,110 L elements: 5,106 outside NOTE ancestry and four Latin lines quoted within two chapter-19 notes. The direct poem total separates into 4,922 witness-derived Middle English lines, eighty-four Huth-created Middle English lines, and one hundred Latin supplement lines.

Huth's two Middle English repairs comprise eighty-two consecutive fully italic lines for a leaf missing from the current witness and two italic lines where the exemplar omitted text but the scribe left space. These are editor reconstructions, not manuscript readings.

Huth's one-hundred-line Latin block on printed pp. 154-157 derives from a later supplement now at ff. 65r-65v, Yale canvases 133-134. It crosses the first five sorrow units of chapter 44 and is not evidence that Latin is an intellectual language of the English translation.

Yale calls the item completely digitized and publishes 144 canvases, including covers, edges, endpapers, text leaves, and blanks. Cache completeness is therefore relative to every provider-published canvas; physical completeness remains qualified until the provider labels are reconciled with the 64-leaf collation.

Yale's manifest says single columns of seventeen lines, while Christie's detailed description and the images show forty-line text pages. The manifest's call-number value 'Takamiya MS 97 1' and discontinuous folio-like labels are preserved as provider data rather than silently normalized.

Huth compared several Latin versions but said the translation could not be assigned to any one of those exact versions. The abstract Latin work is modeled as the source tradition; no surviving Latin witness or print is asserted as the physical translation exemplar.

The pinned CME root LICENSE uses Unlicense language framed expressly as software. Its applicability to corpus XML, data, or markup is unresolved, so no high-confidence repository-wide public-domain claim is inferred from it.

later Latin supply used by Huth = current ff. 65r-65v = Yale canvases 133-134 = OIDs 16157475-16157476 = ZIP images/000133.jpg-images/000134.jpg
manuscript index omitted from CME00118 = current ff. 63r-64r = Yale canvases 129-131
damaged envoy omitted from CME00118 = current f. 64v = Yale canvas 132
labeled blank leaves after Latin supply = [66]r-[66]v = Yale canvases 135-136
notes: The start URL is the incipit at principal-work canvas 7; the end URL is the end of the noncontiguous Latin supply at canvas 134. Both returned HTTP 200 during review.

The principal English work itself ends at canvas 128, f. 62v: https://collections.library.yale.edu/iiif/2/16157470/full/1600,/0/default.jpg . The Latin supply starts at canvas 133: https://collections.library.yale.edu/iiif/2/16157475/full/1600,/0/default.jpg . Both returned HTTP 200.

All 144 canvases exposed by the provider manifest were captured with non-upscaling 1600-pixel-wide IIIF requests, with canonical manifest, inventory, member SHA-256 values, and image files retained in the ZIP.

Complete means complete relative to the provider-published 144-canvas sequence and Yale's 'Completely digitized' claim. The canvas-label sequence is discontinuous and must still be reconciled with the physical 64-leaf collation.

rights.basis: The exact Yale manifest has no top-level IIIF rights URI and instead states that use of each image may be subject to U.S. copyright, site-license, or other rights-management terms and that the user is responsible. Beinecke's current policy says it usually does not own copyright, welcomes public-domain use, requires source credit, and makes users responsible for rights and applicable terms.
rights.component: Yale digital page images, IIIF delivery, provider PDF, and associated site content
rights.jurisdiction: Provider item statement and website terms
notes: Do not substitute the medieval text's public-domain status for an unqualified claim about Yale's modern image files. The item supplies a caution, not a Creative Commons license or Public Domain Mark.

rights.component: DIMEV v1.0 data and project files

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan CME00118, an encoding of the title page and printed pp. 1-166 from Alfred Henry Huth's 1888 Roxburghe Club edition. Huth transcribed the unique surviving Middle English witness, now Yale Beinecke Takamiya MS 97, but supplied eighty-four Middle English lines of his own at two lacunae and substituted one hundred Latin lines copied from a later supplement at current ff. 65r-65v for another missing English passage. The XML omits Huth's preliminaries, manuscript index, damaged envoy, glossary, and endmatter. Complete local derivatives of Huth's 218-page Internet Archive object, the complete DIMEV v1.0 release, and Yale's 144-canvas provider-published IIIF sequence are cached with fixity. The Yale work portion is explicitly noncontiguous: principal English work canvases 7-128 and Latin supply canvases 133-134; index and envoy canvases 129-132 are outside the XML selection.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator
catalogSummary.date: Circa 1400; unique surviving witness circa 1450; source edition 1888
catalogSummary.region: Probably England; the surviving witness was produced in England
catalogSummary.title: The Mirour of Mans Saluacioun

catalogingSubject.label: The Mirour of Mans Saluacioun
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English verse translation of the Latin Speculum humanae salvationis, not the Latin source work, Takamiya MS 97 as a physical object, Alfred Henry Huth's complete 1888 Roxburghe Club volume, the University of Michigan encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact. The represented text is Huth's title page and printed pp. 1-166: the proem, forty-five chapters, and conclusion, including eighty-four Middle English lines reconstructed by Huth and one hundred Latin lines copied from a later manuscript supplement.

contentParts.label: Huth source title page

locators.value: FRONT/DIV1[@TYPE='title page']; four P; PB REF='1'

contentParts.summary: Four paragraphs transcribing the 1888 volume's title, description of the translation and manuscript, preface-and-glossary statement, place, and date.

contentParts.label: Verse proem

locators.value: begins f. 1r; proem ends f. 8v according to DIMEV

contentParts.summary: A 304-line proem invoking the Trinity and Virgin, announcing the translation, and previewing the matter of the forty-five chapters.

contentParts.label: Chapters 1-42

locators.value: DIV2[@TYPE='chapter'][@N='1'] through DIV2[@N='42']; 4,174 L outside NOTE

notes: This count includes Huth's eighty-four reconstructed Middle English lines: eighty-two consecutive italic lines replacing a leaf missing from the current witness and two italic lines replacing an exemplar omission for which the scribe left space.
contentParts.summary: The main typological sequence, treating Creation, the Fall, the Virgin, Christ's life and Passion, Resurrection, Ascension, judgment, and associated prefigurations.

contentParts.label: Chapter 43: the Hours of the Cross
notes: The chapter is one numbered work unit. Its eight nested DIV3 hour units are internal structure, not eight additional numbered items.
contentParts.summary: Twenty-six framing lines followed by eight twenty-six-line prayer units associated with the canonical hours.

contentParts.label: Chapter 44: the seven sorrows of the Virgin

locators.value: English chapter in ff. 1r-62v; later Latin supply at ff. 65r-65v, Yale canvases 133-134
DIV2[@TYPE='chapter'][@N='44']; seven DIV3[@TYPE='sorrow']; direct poem lines 4723-4822 are Latin

notes: The chapter is one numbered work unit. Its seven nested DIV3 sorrow units are internal structure, not seven additional numbered items.
The Latin block is transmission/editorial support, not evidence that Latin is an intellectual language of the English translation.
contentParts.summary: Twenty-six framing lines followed by seven twenty-six-line sorrow units; the represented chapter contains a one-hundred-line Latin substitute where the English exemplar was lacunose.

contentParts.label: Chapter 45: the seven joys of the Virgin
notes: The chapter is one numbered work unit. Its seven nested DIV3 joy units are internal structure, not seven additional numbered items.
contentParts.summary: Twenty-six framing lines followed by seven twenty-six-line joy units.

contentParts.label: Four-line conclusion
contentParts.summary: The translator names the work, calls the rendering a rude translation, and asks grace for hearers and readers.

contentParts.label: Huth's encoded notes and marginalia

locators.value: marginal and foot-of-page notes throughout pp. 1-166
1,199 NOTE; four L nested in two NOTE elements in chapter 19

notes: Not every NOTE is modern prose: many preserve manuscript marginal labels or citations, and two notes contain verse quotations. The notes remain an editorial layer rather than intrinsic verse extent.
contentParts.summary: A network of 1,199 foot and marginal notes supplying glosses, biblical references, manuscript annotations, textual explanation, and four Latin verse lines quoted within two chapter-19 notes.

assertion.notes: Arlima dates the English version circa 1400; this is a work-level estimate, not the circa-1450 date of the surviving copy.
The date belongs to the unique surviving manuscript witness, not necessarily the translation event.

extent.description: The XML has two DIV1 sections: a four-paragraph source title page and the poem. The poem contains 47 DIV2 units (one 304-line proem, forty-five numbered chapters, and a four-line conclusion), 22 nested DIV3 devotional units, 5,106 L elements outside NOTE ancestry, and four Latin L elements inside two notes. Chapters 1-42 contain 4,174 direct lines; chapters 43-45 contain 208 each. Across the file are 167 PB, 1,199 NOTE, ten MILESTONE, 3,452 italic HI, 1,235 SUP, forty CLOSER, and three GAP elements. These are representation metrics, not a claim about an unreconstructed authorial text.

formStatements.description: A long Middle English translation in rhyming couplets, organized as a proem, forty-five chapters, and a four-line conclusion; chapters 43-45 contain nested devotional cycles.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The one-hundred-line Latin block in encoded chapter 44 is a later manuscript supplement used by Huth to bridge an English lacuna; Latin is therefore not promoted to a language of the cataloged English work.
TEXT/@LANG='enm' accurately describes the intellectual language of the medieval translation despite Huth's embedded Latin supply and Latin quotations.
The TEMPHEAD revision date 8-23-16 and MURP proofing note describe encoding history, not work, manuscript, or edition dates.
DIMEV's pp. 1-124 locus is the manuscript's modern pencil pagination, while Yale's current canvas labels use folio-like labels; neither system is silently substituted for the other.
Thomas Cowper and Thomas Wilkinson remain provenance/annotation names, not authors: contradictory late notes and Huth's hand analysis do not establish either as translator or scribe.

next_steps: Consult Avril Henry's 1986 critical edition and later work-specific linguistic and source studies.
Collate the translation against current Latin-recension scholarship without treating Huth's Zainer print, Berjeau block-book reproduction, or circa-1370 Huth manuscript as the demonstrated exemplar.
Keep the unidentified Latin source tradition distinct from the damaged exemplar copied by Takamiya MS 97.
openQuestions.question: What exact date, English region, Latin recension, and physical exemplar underlie the Middle English translation?

next_steps: Compare the italic reconstructions against Huth's three named Latin comparison sources and Henry's 1986 apparatus.
Do not attribute the eighty-four lines to Takamiya MS 97 or to one exact Latin exemplar without passage-level evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which exact Latin versions and editorial decisions supplied Huth's eighty-four reconstructed Middle English lines?

next_steps: Ask Beinecke cataloging staff whether '17 lines' and call number 'Takamiya MS 97 1' are migration errors.

Reconcile each provider canvas label with the manuscript's medieval foliation and modern pencil pagination before citing canvas labels as physical foliation beyond the visually confirmed work boundaries.
openQuestions.question: How should Yale's current folio-like canvas labels and seventeen-line layout statement be reconciled with the 64-leaf physical extent, Christie's detailed collation, forty-line layout, missing folio 25, duplicated 45, and misnumbered verso 50?

assertion.notes: England is a cautious work-level association inferred from the Middle English translation and the English production of its unique surviving witness; it is not a precise localization.
This responsibility is for the medieval English translation, not the anonymous compilation of the Latin source work.

scope.description: All modern source-edition layers represented by CME00118

assertion.notes: Huth calls these his own Middle English versions and prints them in italics; they are not readings from Takamiya MS 97.

scope.description: Eighty-four reconstructed Middle English lines: one block of eighty-two lines and one block of two lines

subjects.label: Life of Christ and the Virgin Mary

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English verse translation of the Speculum humanae salvationis. Its typological chapters align episodes from the lives of the Virgin and Christ with Old Testament, legendary, and ancient-historical prefigurations to explain Christian salvation; its final chapters develop the Hours of the Cross, the Virgin's seven sorrows, and her seven joys.

CME00118 preserves Huth's source title page and the principal edited poem on printed pp. 1-166. It omits the dedication, preface, preliminary word list, errata, manuscript plate, manuscript index on pp. 167-170, damaged Middle English envoy on p. 171, glossary on pp. 173-177, and printer/endmatter.

The XML contains 5,110 L elements, but only 5,106 are direct poem lines: four Latin lines are quoted inside two editorial notes in chapter 19. Of the 5,106 direct lines, 4,922 transcribe the surviving Middle English witness, eighty-four are Huth's own reconstructed Middle English lines, and one hundred are a later Latin supplement copied at current ff. 65r-65v and substituted by Huth for the English lacuna on printed pp. 154-157.

assertion.notes: Spelling varies across the manuscript explicit, Huth's edition, Yale's catalog, DIMEV, and later scholarship; this form is a stable modern scholarly title, not a claim about one authorial spelling.

titles.supplied_by: Modern scholarly usage represented by Avril Henry's critical-edition title

assertion.notes: Huth's 1888 source-edition title form; the manuscript conclusion also names the book 'miroure of mans saluacionne'.

titles.value: In name of God almyghti / þe blyssed Trinitee

And thus eendes right here / this ruyde Translacionne; Off the boke named miroure / of mans saluacionne; Jhū alle thas encreece / in his grace and mercy; Whilk to lerne to do wele / heres or redes it hertly
